content.text: Amazon Web Services is the dominant public-cloud platform, offering over 200 managed services that span compute, storage, networking, databases, AI/ML, and security. For teams building agentic-AI systems, AWS provides every infrastructure layer an agent needs — from scalable container runtimes and durable object storage to fully managed LLM APIs via Amazon Bedrock — all wired together with fine-grained identity and access management.
What it is

AWS is a pay-as-you-go, on-demand infrastructure platform operated by Amazon. Rather than owning physical servers, you provision virtual resources through APIs, the AWS Management Console, or Infrastructure-as-Code tools such as Terraform and AWS CDK. Resources are organised into regions — geographic clusters such as us-east-1 or eu-west-1 — each containing two or more availability zones (AZs) that provide independent fault domains within the same city.

Every AWS interaction is an API call authenticated by IAM (Identity and Access Management). IAM issues identities — users, roles, and service principals — and attaches JSON policies that enumerate exactly which actions are permitted on which resources. Billing is usage-based: you pay per vCPU-hour for EC2, per GB-month for S3, per request for Lambda, and so on. There are no upfront commitments in the default on-demand tier, though Reserved Instances and Savings Plans reduce costs for predictable workloads.

The services most relevant to agentic-AI stacks are: ECS Fargate and EKS for container orchestration; EC2 for bare-metal or GPU workloads; RDS and Aurora for relational state; ElastiCache for Redis-based caching and queues; S3 for object storage; SQS for durable messaging; Secrets Manager for credential storage; CloudWatch for observability; and Amazon Bedrock for managed LLM inference.
Why it matters

Agentic systems have unusual infrastructure requirements. A single agent run may last seconds or hours, spawn sub-agents, call dozens of external tools, write multi-megabyte traces, and retry on transient errors — all while the orchestrating process must remain available to the end user. Managing this on raw virtual machines requires deep operational expertise. AWS managed services absorb the hard parts: RDS handles replication and failover, ECS Fargate eliminates OS patching, SQS provides at-least-once delivery without you operating a broker, and Secrets Manager rotates credentials automatically.

Beyond operational convenience, AWS matters for security posture. Autonomous agents that call external APIs, write to databases, and access confidential documents need strict permission boundaries. IAM task roles scope each container to the minimum permissions it needs, VPC security groups control network traffic at the packet level, and CloudTrail logs every API call for audit. These controls are difficult to replicate on self-managed infrastructure and essential for production deployments.
Benefits
Service breadth
— a managed service for every agent stack layer: compute, messaging, storage, cache, relational DB, vector DB (OpenSearch Serverless), and LLM inference (Bedrock).
Elastic scaling
— ECS Fargate and Application Auto Scaling adjust task counts in minutes based on CPU, memory, or custom CloudWatch metrics like SQS depth.
Fine-grained IAM
— least-privilege roles per container; no static credentials on worker nodes; automatic temporary credential rotation via the EC2 metadata service.
Global infrastructure
— 33+ regions; deploy agents close to users to reduce latency and satisfy data-residency requirements.
Managed reliability
— RDS Multi-AZ, S3 11-nines durability, SQS at-least-once delivery, and ElastiCache replication reduce the ops burden on agent teams.
Integrated observability
— CloudWatch Logs, Metrics, and X-Ray traces available with minimal SDK instrumentation.
Bedrock native path
— run Claude, Titan, or Llama models inside the same cloud account; no data leaves your VPC for inference.
Trade-offs
Complexity
— 200+ services with overlapping use cases; choosing among ECS, EKS, Lambda, and App Runner for a new agent service requires deliberate evaluation.
Cost surprises
— NAT Gateway charges ($0.045/GB outbound), data transfer between AZs, idle RDS instances, and oversized ElastiCache nodes frequently inflate bills unexpectedly.
Vendor lock-in
— Bedrock, SQS, DynamoDB, and many other services use proprietary APIs. Without abstraction layers, migrating to GCP or Azure is expensive.
IAM complexity
— policies, roles, trust relationships, permission boundaries, and Service Control Policies create a large attack surface if misconfigured.
Cold-start latency
— Lambda cold starts and ECS task launch times (30–120s for Fargate) can affect agent responsiveness when scaling from zero.
Operational overhead at large scale
— EKS cluster management, VPC networking, and multi-account setups still require dedicated platform engineering.
How it works
Regions and availability zones

Each AWS region is an isolated geographic area (e.g., us-east-1 in Northern Virginia). Inside a region, 2–6 availability zones each host independent data centers with separate power, cooling, and networking. Resources spread across multiple AZs survive the failure of an entire data center. S3 replicates objects across at least 3 AZs automatically; RDS Multi-AZ maintains a synchronous standby in a second AZ and promotes it in ~60 seconds on primary failure.
IAM: the permission fabric

Every AWS API call carries a signature derived from an IAM principal's credentials. IAM evaluates an allow/deny decision by checking all policies attached to the caller — identity-based policies on the role, resource-based policies on the target (e.g., an S3 bucket policy), and optional permission boundaries. The default is implicit deny; an explicit deny anywhere overrides all allows.

For ECS tasks, the IAM task role is assumed automatically by the container via the ECS metadata endpoint (http://169.254.170.2). The AWS SDK fetches temporary credentials from this endpoint transparently. This means containers never need long-lived access keys baked into environment variables.
Networking: VPC, subnets, and security groups

A Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) is a private network inside a region. You divide it into subnets across AZs. Public subnets have a route to an Internet Gateway; private subnets route outbound traffic through a NAT Gateway (for IPv4). Data stores — RDS, ElastiCache, OpenSearch — must live in private subnets with no direct internet route. ECS tasks in private subnets can reach the internet for API calls but are unreachable from the internet unless placed behind a load balancer. Security groups act as stateful firewalls: you specify which ports and source CIDRs are allowed inbound and outbound for each resource.
Service interaction model

Applications running on ECS call AWS services via the AWS SDK (boto3, AWS SDK for JavaScript, etc.). The SDK resolves regional endpoints (e.g., s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com), signs requests with SigV4 using the task role credentials, and sends HTTPS requests. Responses are returned synchronously for simple reads/writes; long-running operations (RDS backups, ECR image scans) use async polling patterns.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a reference architecture for an agentic-AI platform on AWS. The agent API containers handle incoming requests and enqueue long-running jobs to SQS. Worker containers poll SQS and call Amazon Bedrock for LLM inference. Both container types run inside a VPC and access data stores through private subnet endpoints only. IAM task roles grant each container the minimum permissions required.
Component roles
ALB (Application Load Balancer)
— terminates TLS, routes HTTP/2 to ECS agent-api tasks, performs health checks, and handles weighted target groups for canary deploys.
ECS Fargate (agent-api)
— stateless FastAPI or Flask containers; autoscale 2–10 tasks on CPU; push jobs to SQS and write session metadata to RDS.
ECS Fargate (worker)
— long-running Celery or custom worker containers; pull from SQS, call Bedrock, write traces to S3, update run status in RDS.
Amazon Bedrock
— managed LLM inference; no model hosting overhead; supports Claude 3 Sonnet/Haiku, Amazon Titan, and Llama 3 via a unified API.
SQS
— durable FIFO or standard queue; decouples API from workers; visibility timeout prevents duplicate processing.
RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ
— stores sessions, runs, and tool_events; synchronous standby for failover; automated backups to S3 with point-in-time recovery.
ElastiCache Redis
— session cache, rate-limit counters, and Celery broker/result backend; cluster mode for horizontal sharding.
S3
— LLM traces, uploaded documents, eval artifacts; 11-nines durability; lifecycle rules to Glacier after 90 days.
Secrets Manager
— OPENAI_API_KEY, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, RDS password; rotated automatically; fetched at container startup, not baked into images.
CloudWatch
— structured logs from all containers; custom metrics for agent tool calls and errors; alarms trigger SNS notifications.
IAM task roles
— one role per service; scoped to required S3 prefixes, SQS queues, and Secrets Manager ARNs.
When to use it

AWS is the right choice when an organisation already has an AWS footprint, uses Bedrock for LLM access, or needs the breadth of managed services that AWS provides. It is particularly well-suited to teams that want a single cloud for the full agent stack — compute, storage, messaging, LLMs, and observability — without managing cross-provider networking.
Use ECS Fargate
when the team is familiar with Docker and wants serverless container orchestration without managing EC2 instances or Kubernetes control planes.
Use EKS
when the workload requires Kubernetes-native features — custom operators, Helm charts, sidecar injection, or GPU node pools for fine-tuning.
Use Lambda
for event-driven, short-duration agent steps (< 15 min) that fire on S3 events, SQS messages, or API Gateway requests; avoid it for long-running multi-turn agent loops.
Use Bedrock
when LLM inference must stay within the AWS account boundary for data-privacy reasons, or when avoiding cross-cloud egress charges matters.
Prefer GCP or Azure
if the team already has deep expertise there or if specific services — such as GCP's Vertex AI or Azure OpenAI — offer better model access or pricing for a given use case.
How to implement
Step 1 — Create the VPC and subnets

Use Terraform or AWS CDK to provision a VPC with at least two public subnets (for the ALB) and two private subnets (for ECS tasks and data stores) spread across two AZs. Add a NAT Gateway in each public subnet for outbound internet access from private subnets.

# Terraform excerpt
module "vpc" {
  source  = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "5.0.0"

  name = "agent-prod"
  cidr = "10.0.0.0/16"

  azs             = ["us-east-1a", "us-east-1b"]
  public_subnets  = ["10.0.1.0/24", "10.0.2.0/24"]
  private_subnets = ["10.0.3.0/24", "10.0.4.0/24"]

  enable_nat_gateway = true
  single_nat_gateway = false  # one per AZ for HA

  tags = { Env = "prod", Team = "agent" }
}
Step 2 — Push a Docker image to ECR

# Authenticate Docker to your private ECR registry
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 |   docker login --username AWS   --password-stdin 123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Build and push
docker build -t agent-api:v1.2.3 .
docker tag agent-api:v1.2.3   123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/agent-api:v1.2.3
docker push   123456789012.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/agent-api:v1.2.3
Step 3 — Define an ECS task with an IAM task role
Step 4 — Register a minimal IAM task role policy
Step 5 — Deploy and roll out

# Force a new deployment (pulls the latest task definition revision)
aws ecs update-service   --cluster agent-prod   --service agent-api   --force-new-deployment   --region us-east-1

# Watch the deployment stabilise
aws ecs wait services-stable   --cluster agent-prod   --services agent-api   --region us-east-1

echo "Deployment complete"
Examples
Example 1 — Reading a secret and writing to S3 from Python
This example shows the core boto3 pattern used in an agent worker: fetch a secret at startup, then write a trace object to S3 using the IAM task role (no explicit credentials needed).

import json
import boto3
from botocore.exceptions import ClientError

# boto3 automatically uses the ECS task role via the metadata endpoint.
# No access keys in environment variables.

def get_secret(secret_arn: str) -> str:
    client = boto3.client("secretsmanager", region_name="us-east-1")
    response = client.get_secret_value(SecretId=secret_arn)
    return response["SecretString"]


def save_trace(run_id: str, trace: dict, bucket: str = "agent-traces-prod") -> str:
    """Upload a JSON trace to S3 and return the object key."""
    s3 = boto3.client("s3", region_name="us-east-1")
    key = f"traces/{run_id}/trace.json"
    s3.put_object(
        Bucket=bucket,
        Key=key,
        Body=json.dumps(trace, indent=2).encode(),
        ContentType="application/json",
    )
    return key


def poll_sqs(queue_url: str, max_messages: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
    """Receive up to max_messages from an SQS queue."""
    sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
    response = sqs.receive_message(
        QueueUrl=queue_url,
        MaxNumberOfMessages=max_messages,
        WaitTimeSeconds=20,  # long-poll to reduce empty receives
        AttributeNames=["All"],
    )
    return response.get("Messages", [])


def delete_message(queue_url: str, receipt_handle: str) -> None:
    sqs = boto3.client("sqs", region_name="us-east-1")
    sqs.delete_message(QueueUrl=queue_url, ReceiptHandle=receipt_handle)


# --- Worker loop ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    QUEUE_URL = "https://sqs.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/123456789012/agent-jobs"
    SECRET_ARN = "arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456789012:secret:agent/anthropic-key"

    api_key = get_secret(SECRET_ARN)
    print(f"API key length: {len(api_key)}")

    messages = poll_sqs(QUEUE_URL)
    for msg in messages:
        body = json.loads(msg["Body"])
        run_id = body["run_id"]
        print(f"Processing run {run_id}")

        # ... call Bedrock or Anthropic API, build trace ...
        trace = {"run_id": run_id, "steps": [], "status": "completed"}
        key = save_trace(run_id, trace)
        print(f"Trace saved to s3://agent-traces-prod/{key}")

        delete_message(QUEUE_URL, msg["ReceiptHandle"])
        print(f"Message deleted from queue")
Example 2 — Calling Amazon Bedrock (Claude) from a Fargate container
Bedrock provides a unified converse API that supports all major foundation models. The container assumes a task role with bedrock:InvokeModel permission; no API key management is needed.

import json
import boto3

def call_bedrock_claude(
    prompt: str,
    model_id: str = "anthropic.claude-3-sonnet-20240229-v1:0",
    max_tokens: int = 1024,
    region: str = "us-east-1",
) -> str:
    """
    Invoke Claude on Amazon Bedrock using the ECS task role.
    Required IAM permission: bedrock:InvokeModel on the model ARN.
    """
    client = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name=region)

    body = {
        "anthropic_version": "bedrock-2023-05-31",
        "max_tokens": max_tokens,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    }

    response = client.invoke_model(
        modelId=model_id,
        contentType="application/json",
        accept="application/json",
        body=json.dumps(body).encode(),
    )

    result = json.loads(response["body"].read())
    return result["content"][0]["text"]


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Works from any ECS Fargate container with the right task role.
    answer = call_bedrock_claude(
        "List the three most important AWS services for running a Python agent. "
        "Be concise — one sentence per service."
    )
    print(answer)
Real-world example

A B2B SaaS company runs a document-analysis agent that processes legal contracts uploaded by customers. The architecture uses ECS Fargate with two services: a REST API (2–10 tasks, autoscaled on CPU) and a worker pool (4 tasks, autoscaled on SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible).

When a customer uploads a contract through the web app, the API validates the file, writes it to an S3 prefix scoped to the customer's account ID, and enqueues a job to SQS with the S3 key and a run ID. A worker task picks up the message, fetches the document from S3, calls Claude via Bedrock to extract clauses, writes structured output back to RDS (run_results table), and stores the full reasoning trace to S3. If Bedrock returns a throttling error, the SQS message becomes visible again after the visibility timeout (30s), implementing automatic retry with backoff.

RDS runs PostgreSQL 15 Multi-AZ with a db.t4g.medium instance. Connection pooling is handled by PgBouncer running as a sidecar in each ECS task, keeping the connection count below RDS's per-instance limit. ElastiCache Redis caches frequently-requested clause summaries for 10 minutes, reducing redundant Bedrock calls by roughly 40% in production.

Secrets Manager stores the RDS password and the Anthropic API key (used as a fallback when Bedrock model availability is degraded). A Lambda function triggered nightly by EventBridge enforces an S3 lifecycle policy, moving objects older than 90 days to Glacier Instant Retrieval to contain storage costs. CloudWatch alarms page the on-call engineer when the SQS queue depth exceeds 500 messages for 5 minutes, indicating worker saturation.
Common mistakes
Using long-lived access keys instead of IAM roles

A common pattern in early prototypes is setting AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID and AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY as environment variables in ECS task definitions. These keys are stored in plaintext in the task definition (visible in the AWS console) and do not rotate automatically. The correct pattern is to assign an IAM task role; the ECS metadata endpoint provides temporary credentials that rotate every 6 hours.

# Wrong: static key in task definition env
"environment": [
  {"name": "AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID", "value": "AKIA..."},
  {"name": "AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY", "value": "secret..."}
]

# Right: task role — no static keys anywhere
"taskRoleArn": "arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/agent-worker-role"
# boto3 picks up credentials automatically from the metadata endpoint
Public S3 buckets and missing bucket policies

S3 buckets are private by default in new accounts, but the Block Public Access setting can be disabled at the account or bucket level. Always enable Block Public Access at the account level and add a bucket policy that denies any s3:GetObject request where the principal is not within the account.
No S3 lifecycle policy — unbounded storage growth

Agent traces are append-only and grow indefinitely. Without lifecycle rules, a busy agent can accumulate terabytes of trace data costing thousands of dollars per month. Add lifecycle rules at bucket creation that transition objects to Standard-IA after 30 days, Glacier Instant Retrieval after 90 days, and delete after 365 days (adjust to compliance requirements).
Forgetting NAT Gateway charges

NAT Gateway costs $0.045 per GB processed in us-east-1. An agent that downloads large model artifacts, fetches documents, or streams large API responses through a NAT Gateway can incur unexpected data charges. Audit outbound data volumes before going to production and consider VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB (free within a VPC) to eliminate NAT costs for those services.
No cost allocation tags

Without consistent tagging (e.g., Environment, Team, TenantID), it is impossible to attribute AWS spend to specific tenants or services in Cost Explorer. Apply tags at resource creation via Terraform and enforce them with AWS Config rules.
Best practices
Infrastructure as Code everywhere
— use Terraform or AWS CDK for all resources; never click-ops production changes. Store state in S3 + DynamoDB lock table.
Separate accounts per environment
— use AWS Organizations with distinct accounts for dev, staging, and prod to prevent accidental cross-environment access and isolate blast radius.
One IAM task role per service
— agent-api, worker, and migration jobs each get their own role scoped to exactly the resources they touch.
Enable CloudTrail in all regions
— log all management and data events to a dedicated S3 bucket in a security account; enable log file integrity validation.
Use VPC endpoints for S3 and Secrets Manager
— eliminate NAT Gateway data charges and keep traffic within the AWS network.
Set billing alerts
— configure a Budget with SNS alerts at 50%, 80%, and 100% of the monthly forecast. Include per-service anomaly detection.
Pin container image tags to digests in production
— use sha256 digests instead of :latest to guarantee reproducible deployments.
Enable RDS Performance Insights and Enhanced Monitoring
— diagnose slow queries from agent database writes before they affect user-facing latency.
Use Fargate Spot for worker tasks
— worker tasks that tolerate interruption (they re-read from SQS on restart) can run at 70% discount on Spot capacity.
Performance & scalability
Autoscaling ECS services

ECS Application Auto Scaling supports target tracking and step scaling policies. For the API service, scale on CPU utilisation targeting 60% — this leaves headroom for request spikes before new tasks are added. For the worker service, scale on a custom CloudWatch metric derived from SQS ApproximateNumberOfMessagesVisible divided by the desired task count, targeting a backlog per task of 10 messages.

import boto3

def configure_worker_autoscaling(cluster: str, service: str, queue_url: str) -> None:
    aas = boto3.client("application-autoscaling", region_name="us-east-1")
    cloudwatch = boto3.client("cloudwatch", region_name="us-east-1")

    # Register the ECS service as a scalable target
    aas.register_scalable_target(
        ServiceNamespace="ecs",
        ResourceId=f"service/{cluster}/{service}",
        ScalableDimension="ecs:service:DesiredCount",
        MinCapacity=2,
        MaxCapacity=20,
    )

    # Step scaling based on SQS backlog per task
    aas.put_scaling_policy(
        PolicyName="worker-sqs-backlog",
        ServiceNamespace="ecs",
        ResourceId=f"service/{cluster}/{service}",
        ScalableDimension="ecs:service:DesiredCount",
        PolicyType="StepScaling",
        StepScalingPolicyConfiguration={
            "AdjustmentType": "ChangeInCapacity",
            "Cooldown": 60,
            "StepAdjustments": [
                {"MetricIntervalLowerBound": 0, "MetricIntervalUpperBound": 50, "ScalingAdjustment": 1},
                {"MetricIntervalLowerBound": 50, "ScalingAdjustment": 3},
            ],
        },
    )
    print(f"Autoscaling configured for {service}")
Database connection management

Each ECS task holds a connection pool to RDS. With 10 API tasks each holding 10 connections, the database already has 100 connections — near the limit for a db.t4g.medium (max_connections ~ 170). Run PgBouncer in transaction-mode pooling as a sidecar container on each task to multiplex many application connections over a few database connections. Alternatively, use RDS Proxy, which manages connection pooling as a managed service and also integrates with Secrets Manager for credential rotation.
S3 throughput

S3 supports at least 3,500 PUT/s and 5,500 GET/s per prefix per account. If the agent produces many small trace objects, aggregate them into larger payloads before writing (e.g., batch 100 spans into one JSON array). For high-throughput scenarios, spread objects across multiple prefixes by hashing the key prefix.
SQS and message ordering

Standard SQS queues offer at-least-once delivery with no ordering guarantee — suitable for most agent job queues. FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and ordering within a message group, but cap throughput at 3,000 messages/s per queue (with batching). For most agentic workloads, a standard queue with idempotent message handlers is both simpler and faster.
Tooling & ecosystem
boto3 (Python)
— the official AWS SDK for Python; used for all programmatic resource access. Install with pip install boto3.
AWS CDK
— define AWS infrastructure in Python, TypeScript, or Go; synthesises to CloudFormation. Preferred for teams writing Python agent code.
Terraform (+ AWS provider)
— HCL-based IaC; provider version 5.x supports all modern AWS services. Strong community modules for VPC, ECS, RDS.
AWS CLI v2
— aws command-line tool for one-off operations, CI scripts, and local debugging. Install via the official installer or Homebrew.
Amazon Bedrock SDK
— part of boto3 (bedrock-runtime client); supports Claude, Titan, Llama 3, and Mistral models via InvokeModel and Converse APIs.
AWS X-Ray
— distributed tracing; SDKs for Python and Node.js automatically propagate trace context across Lambda, ECS, and API Gateway.
AWS Copilot CLI
— opinionated CLI that scaffolds ECS Fargate services and pipelines; good for small teams that want sensible defaults without writing raw Terraform.
Localstack
— runs AWS APIs locally in Docker for integration testing without incurring real AWS charges; supports S3, SQS, DynamoDB, Secrets Manager, and more.
Checkov
— static analysis for Terraform and CloudFormation; enforces security best practices (public S3, unencrypted RDS, overbroad IAM) in CI.

# Install and test locally with Localstack
pip install localstack awscli-local

# Start Localstack
localstack start -d

# Create a local S3 bucket (uses awslocal wrapper)
awslocal s3 mb s3://agent-traces-local
awslocal s3 cp trace.json s3://agent-traces-local/traces/run-1/trace.json
awslocal s3 ls s3://agent-traces-local/traces/
Comparison with alternatives
The table below compares AWS with Google Cloud Platform and Microsoft Azure for agentic-AI workloads across the most commonly evaluated dimensions.
Dimension
AWS
Google Cloud (GCP)
Azure
Container orchestration
ECS Fargate / EKS
Cloud Run / GKE
Azure Container Apps / AKS
Managed LLM inference
Amazon Bedrock (Claude, Titan, Llama)
Vertex AI (Gemini, Claude)
Azure OpenAI Service (GPT-4, o1)
Object storage
S3 — 11-nines durability, mature lifecycle policies
Cloud Storage — similar SLA, strong streaming
Azure Blob Storage — tiering comparable to S3
Relational database
RDS / Aurora PostgreSQL — Multi-AZ, RDS Proxy
Cloud SQL / AlloyDB — strong PG compatibility
Azure Database for PostgreSQL — Flexible Server
Message queue
SQS — simple, unlimited throughput (standard)
Pub/Sub — push/pull, ordering via ordering keys
Service Bus — sessions, dead-letter, FIFO support
IAM model
Fine-grained JSON policies, task roles, SCPs
Workload identity, IAM bindings, org policies
RBAC + managed identities, Azure Policy
Observability
CloudWatch Logs + Metrics + X-Ray
Cloud Logging + Cloud Monitoring + Cloud Trace
Azure Monitor + Application Insights
Global regions (2025)
33 regions, 105 AZs
40 regions, 121 zones
60+ regions worldwide
Free tier
12 months on many services + always-free tier
Always-free tier for most services
12 months + $200 credit
Pricing complexity
High — many per-resource dimensions
Medium — fewer distinct services
High — similar to AWS
Security & reliability
Defence-in-depth

A production agent platform on AWS uses multiple independent security layers. Network perimeter: VPC with private subnets and security groups reject all traffic not explicitly allowed. Identity layer: IAM task roles with least-privilege policies; deny-all SCP at the AWS Organisation root that blocks common misuse patterns (creating users with passwords, disabling CloudTrail). Application layer: no secrets in environment variables; all credentials fetched from Secrets Manager; Anthropic / OpenAI API keys never logged.
Encryption at rest and in transit

All AWS services used in the agent stack support encryption at rest via AWS KMS. Enable server-side encryption for S3 (SSE-KMS), RDS (KMS CMK), ElastiCache (encryption at rest), and Secrets Manager (uses KMS by default). All service endpoints use TLS 1.2+; enforce minimum TLS version on ALB listeners. For S3, enable enforce-SSL bucket policies that deny unencrypted HTTP requests.
Reliability and fault tolerance

Spread ECS tasks across multiple AZs using the SPREAD placement strategy. Set the ALB health check to fail if the /health endpoint takes more than 5 seconds or returns non-2xx. ECS deregisters unhealthy tasks from the load balancer and replaces them. RDS Multi-AZ provides automatic failover in 60–120 seconds; application code must handle connection retries with exponential backoff. SQS visibility timeouts act as a circuit breaker: if a worker crashes mid-run, the message reappears and is retried by another worker after the timeout elapses.
Audit and compliance

Enable CloudTrail with management events and S3 data events in all regions. Deliver logs to an S3 bucket in a dedicated security account with object lock enabled (WORM) to prevent tampering. Use AWS Config to continuously evaluate resources against rules such as s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited, rds-instance-public-access-check, and iam-no-inline-policy-check. GuardDuty monitors VPC flow logs and CloudTrail for anomalous behaviour — such as an agent container attempting to query the EC2 metadata endpoint for credentials it should not have.
Key takeaways

The core AWS stack for agentic-AI is ECS Fargate (compute), RDS PostgreSQL (run state), ElastiCache Redis (cache/queue), S3 (traces/docs), SQS (job buffer), Secrets Manager (credentials), and CloudWatch (observability).
IAM task roles eliminate long-lived access keys; every container assumes a role scoped to exactly the resources it touches, with credentials rotated automatically by ECS.
Private subnets for all data stores (RDS, ElastiCache, OpenSearch) and security groups that allow only application-layer traffic are the minimum viable network security posture.
Amazon Bedrock provides managed Claude and Titan inference within the AWS account boundary, removing the need for an external API key and keeping LLM traffic off the public internet.
SQS decouples the API from worker containers and provides built-in retry via visibility timeouts — no custom retry logic needed for transient worker failures.
Infrastructure as Code (Terraform or CDK) is mandatory for production; click-ops changes are unauditable, unrepeatable, and frequently the source of security misconfigurations.
The main cost surprises are NAT Gateway data transfer, idle RDS instances, and S3 storage without lifecycle rules — budget alerts and per-resource tagging are essential from day one.
Encrypt everything at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS 1.2+); enable CloudTrail in all regions and AWS Config rules for continuous compliance validation.
